creative_brief: Hero image: overhead shot of a family's weekly grocery haul spread across a kitchen bench, with a small FarmThru branded tote visible. Warm, natural light. Below the fold, use clean infographic-style visuals showing the simplified supply chain (farm → hub → you) contrasted against the traditional chain. Earthy palette, no stock photography — real texture, real food.
headline: You spend $200 a week on groceries. You've never owned a single share of where it goes.
subhead: FarmThru is building a farm-direct grocery store that cuts out unnecessary middlemen — and we're opening ownership to the people who actually buy the food. Starting at $50.
hero_copy:
Think about it. You'll spend six figures on groceries in your lifetime. Every dollar flows through a system you have zero stake in. The farmers growing your food get squeezed. You pay more every quarter. And the middlemen between you and the farm? They do just fine.

What if the next grocery store was built differently — and you could own a piece of it before it scales?

sections.heading: For years, I watched the people growing our food get the worst deal in the chain
sections.body:
I saw farming families price their produce below what it cost to grow. Not by choice — because that's what the system offered them. Meanwhile, families on the other end paid more every year for food that travelled thousands of kilometres before it reached a shelf.

FarmThru exists to fix that broken middle. We partner directly with farms across NSW — around 15 to 20 and growing — and bring their food to our Brookvale hub. Customers order online, collect when it suits them, Monday to Friday. No subscription. No lock-in. No membership fees. Just a shorter, fairer supply chain.

The model is simple: cut the unnecessary middlemen, pass the savings to families, and pay farmers what their work is actually worth.

sections.heading: Why this is an investment thesis, not just a grocery run
sections.body:
Most grocery businesses are built on razor-thin margins propped up by massive logistics costs. FarmThru's hub-and-collect model strips out the infrastructure that bleeds traditional retail dry. No vast store footprints. No home delivery fleet burning cash. Customers buy in bulk, which rewards their commitment and increases affordability.

Farm to hub to customer. That's the supply chain. Every step that doesn't add value has been removed.

Direct-to-consumer food is growing at 24% year-on-year in Australia. The $130B grocery market is shifting — and the brands building trust with transparency are the ones positioned to capture that shift. FarmThru isn't competing on price alone. We're competing on a model the big chains can't replicate without dismantling their own.

sections.heading: Own a piece of the grocery store you'd actually choose
sections.body:
Through equity crowdfunding on Birchal, we're letting everyday customers become owners. Not venture capitalists. Not institutions. The people who care about where their food comes from.

Minimum investment: $50. Maximum: $10,000 per investor.

This isn't without risk. Early-stage businesses can fail. Your capital is not guaranteed. You may lose some or all of what you invest. But if you believe the way Australians buy food is due for a fundamental rethink — and you want to be part of building it rather than watching from the sideline — this is how.

Put down a $5 refundable deposit to join the VIP waitlist. You'll get first access to the campaign, priority updates, and an exclusive Q&A before the public raise opens. No commitment beyond that.

sections.heading: What your $5 refundable deposit gets you
sections.body:
First access when the equity crowdfunding campaign goes live. Priority updates on milestones, farm partnerships, and hub expansion. An exclusive Q&A session with the founding team. And it's fully refundable — if you change your mind, you get it back. No questions.

You're not locked in. You're just first in line.

sections.heading: Compliance
sections.body: FarmThru's equity crowdfunding campaign is conducted through Birchal. This content is not financial advice. Investing in early-stage businesses carries significant risk, including the potential loss of your entire investment. Past performance is not indicative of future results. No returns are projected or guaranteed. You should consider whether this investment is appropriate for your financial situation and seek independent advice if needed. Any investment decision is made at your own risk.
